title: Open, Save, Quit
subtitle: Launch Neovim, write your work, and get out — three ways each
summary: Open a file with `nvim`. Write with `:w`. Quit with `:q`. Combine with `:wq`, `:x`, or `ZZ`. Discard with `:q!` or `ZQ`.

blocks.text: The single most-googled question about Vim is "how do I exit?" Let's get that out of the way first, then make it a real skill.
If the file exists, you see its contents. If it doesn't, you see an empty buffer that *will* be saved as `hello.txt` the first time you write. Vim doesn't create the file on disk until you save.

blocks.label: Write the buffer to its file

blocks.text: {key::}{key:w} "writes" — Vim's word for save. {key::}{key:w} {filename} writes to a different filename (Save As). {key::}{key:w}{key:!} forces a write through read-only flags.
{key::}{key:q} quits — *if* the buffer is clean. If there are unsaved changes, Vim refuses and tells you to save or force-quit. Force-quit with {key::}{key:q}{key:!} discards changes and exits.

rows: Write, then quit. Always writes (even if nothing changed).
Write *if changed*, then quit. Quieter than `:wq`.
Same as `:x` — write-if-changed, then quit. Normal-mode shortcut.

blocks.text: Use {key:Z}{key:Z} as your default "done" key — no colon, no Enter, just two capital Zs. It's also the most ergonomic of the bunch.

If you ever quit with {key::}{key:q}{key:!} and regret it, remember: Vim writes a per-session swapfile. The next time you open the file, Vim may offer to recover. The undo *file* (if you have `'undofile'` set) survives quit, so you can even reopen and undo back to a state before the changes you discarded.

blocks.title: Buffers, Files, and What :w Actually Does
blocks.text:
When you open a file, Vim copies it into memory as a *buffer* — an in-memory list of lines. Your edits change the buffer, not the file. {key::}{key:w} (write) flushes the buffer to disk; until then, the on-disk file is unchanged.

Vim also creates a *swapfile* (`.<filename>.swp`) next to the buffer's file. The swapfile is a periodic snapshot of unsaved changes; if Vim or your machine crashes, the swapfile lets the next `nvim <file>` invocation recover. This is also what causes the "swap file already exists" prompt — usually Vim was killed without exiting cleanly, and the swap is still on disk.

The *undofile* (separate from the swap, controlled by `'undofile'`) persists the undo *tree* across sessions: open a file tomorrow and {key:u} can still walk back through today's edits.

Quitting with {key:Z}{key:Z} (or {key::}{key:x}) writes the buffer if it's modified and exits. Quitting with {key:Z}{key:Q} (or {key::}{key:q}{key:!}) deletes the swapfile and exits without writing — your unsaved buffer changes are gone, but the on-disk file is intact (it never got written this session).

blocks.title: Multiple Files
blocks.text:
Vim can hold many buffers at once. {key::}{key:e} {file} opens *another* file in a new buffer, leaving the previous one in memory. {key::}{key:l}{key:s} lists all loaded buffers. {key::}{key:b} {n} switches to buffer N.

This matters for quitting: {key::}{key:q} closes the current *window*; if other windows or buffers are open, Vim is still running. {key::}{key:q}{key:a} (quit-all) closes everything. {key::}{key:w}{key:q}{key:a} writes-all-then-quits-all. If you've ever pressed {key::}{key:q} expecting Vim to exit and gotten "E37: No write since last change" or seen it switch to another buffer instead — it's because the buffer/window distinction matters even at this level.

Full treatment in `windows.buffers`.
